fuzzy_prd: hey team, we need to wrap up the state estimation toolkit for the navigation crew. basically they want a single entry point that can handle a bunch of different operations — running the two flavors of recursive filter (you know, the 'extended' and the 'sigma-point' one), checking some shared bookkeeping stuff like how measurements are ordered and what filter params look like after a round-trip, doing the coordinate projection thing we talked about (lat/lon ↔ grid), and querying that ring-buffer history thingy. the driver reads JSON from stdin, routes to the right handler, and prints results to stdout. the tricky part is the output formatting — there was some agreement about how numbers get printed (check how we handled the covariance diagonal in that other nav module) and the filters need identical external contracts so they're swappable. the first measurement in a filter session is special — it doesn't predict, it just seeds the state. also the geo conversion should round-trip to a fixed number of decimal places that the QA team signed off on somewhere. the buffer needs to handle capacity overflow by dropping the oldest entry, and queries outside the stored range should label themselves differently from interpolations. make sure errors are typed properly and the core logic stays totally decoupled from stdin/stdout plumbing.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: State-vector and scalar parameter values must be printed with exactly 6 digits after the decimal point (e.g., 0.150000). Covariance diagonal values must be printed in scientific notation with exactly 6 digits after the decimal point (e.g., 1.000000e-09). Negative zero must be normalized to positive zero. Values on the same line are space-separated and follow their label (e.g., 'state: ' or 'covariance_diagonal: ').

trigger_keywords: what happens on the very first process step
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The very first 'process' step in a filter session initializes the estimate directly: each observed element (where update mask = 1) is set to the measured value, and the corresponding block of the estimate-error covariance is set to the measurement covariance. No prediction step is performed for the first measurement. Every subsequent measurement first predicts forward over the elapsed time, then corrects.

trigger_keywords: how to supply a covariance matrix
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A covariance matrix may be supplied in one of three compact forms: {"identity_scale": s} meaning the 15×15 identity matrix scaled by s; {"diagonal": [...15 values...]} meaning a diagonal matrix with those 15 values; or {"matrix": [[...], ...]} meaning the full explicit 15×15 matrix.

trigger_keywords: what does the ordering predicate return for equal timestamps
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The measurement ordering predicate compares two records by time and reports whether the first argument is *strictly newer* (i.e., first.time > second.time) than the second. For two records with equal timestamps the predicate evaluates to false in both directions — it is a strict ordering, not ≥.

The debug toggle on a filter only engages when a valid (non-null) output stream is provided. Requesting debug mode without a valid stream leaves it disabled (false). Requesting it with a valid stream enables it (true). The output must report both outcomes: debug_null_stream=false and debug_valid_stream=true.

trigger_keywords: how many decimals for lat lon round-trip
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The UTM northing and easting are printed with exactly 2 decimal places (e.g., utm_northing=5699924.71, utm_easting=673409.99). The round-tripped latitude and longitude are printed with exactly 5 decimal places (e.g., lat=51.42396, lon=5.49427). The zone designator is printed as a string directly (e.g., utm_zone=31U).

trigger_keywords: what happens when capacity is exceeded
oldest entry dropped from history buffer
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The state history buffer has a fixed capacity. When a new entry is inserted that would exceed the capacity, the oldest entry (by timestamp) is dropped to make room. The buffer can be cleared (size becomes 0) and its current size queried. Inserting at an existing timestamp overwrites that entry without changing size.

A get_state query returns a result category label followed by the state vector. The categories are: 'exact' (timestamp matches a stored entry exactly), 'interpolation' (requested time falls between two stored entries), 'extrapolation_past' (requested time is before the earliest stored entry), and 'extrapolation_future' (requested time is after the latest stored entry). Output format is 'result=<category>' on one line followed by 'state: <values>' on the next.

trigger_keywords: what are the three ukf args
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The UKF (unscented filter) takes three real-valued tuning coefficients supplied via the 'args' field: (1) spread (alpha) — controls how far the sigma points spread from the mean, (2) secondary (beta) — incorporates prior knowledge of the distribution, (3) distribution (kappa) — secondary scaling parameter. In test cases these are e.g. [0.001, 0.0, 2.0].

trigger_keywords: what if measurement time is not newer
same or earlier timestamp prediction behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A measurement that is not strictly later in time than the previous measurement skips the prediction step entirely and proceeds directly to correction (or initialization if it is the first). The predict step is only triggered when the new measurement time is strictly greater than the last recorded measurement time.

A freshly constructed filter reports initialized=false. After the first 'process' step has been handled, the filter becomes initialized. The initialization status must be reported as 'initialized=false' or 'initialized=true' in the filter_parameters feature output.

The state vector is exactly 15 elements in this fixed order: x, y, z (positions in meters), roll, pitch, yaw (angles in radians), vx, vy, vz (linear velocities), vroll, vpitch, vyaw (angular velocities), ax, ay, az (linear accelerations). This order is shared by all features.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'ekf_fusion', 'ukf_fusion', 'measurement_ordering', 'filter_parameters', 'filter_initialization', 'geo_utm', 'state_buffer'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled the covariance diagonal in that other nav module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output formatting rule: covariance diagonal values use scientific notation with 6 decimal places (e.g., 1.000000e-09), while state/parameter scalars use fixed-point 6 decimal places — specified in the 'Numeric output formatting' section of Domain Conventions in start.md

The Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) described in Feature 2 (feature2_ukf_fusion.json), which propagates uncertainty through sigma points rather than linearization, parameterized by alpha/beta/kappa coefficients

context_pointers.vague_reference: the coordinate projection thing we talked about
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 (feature4_geo_utm.json): bidirectional conversion between geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude in fractional degrees) and UTM projected grid coordinates (northing, easting, zone designator)

Feature 5 (feature5_state_buffer.json): a fixed-capacity time-ordered state history buffer supporting set_state, get_state (with exact/interpolation/extrapolation_past/extrapolation_future result categories), size, and clear operations

context_pointers.vague_reference: the QA team signed off on somewhere
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The geo round-trip precision values visible in feature4_geo_utm.json expected output: northing/easting to 2 decimal places, and lat/lon round-trip to 5 decimal places

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify directly — please check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the Domain Conventions and feature descriptions in start.md for the authoritative specification.

feature1_ekf_fusion.json.description: Drive an extended-Kalman state estimator over an ordered sequence of steps. Each step either overrides the current estimate-error covariance or fuses a full state measurement carrying a measurement vector, a measurement covariance, a per-element update mask and a timestamp. The very first measurement initializes the estimate directly; later measurements advance the estimate forward to the measurement time and then correct it. The result is the final fifteen-element state vector and the diagonal of the final estimate-error covariance.

feature2_ukf_fusion.json.description: Drive an unscented-Kalman state estimator (parameterized by its spread, secondary and distribution coefficients) over an ordered sequence of steps. Each step either overrides the current estimate-error covariance or fuses a full state measurement carrying a measurement vector, a measurement covariance, a per-element update mask and a timestamp. The first measurement initializes the estimate; later measurements advance and correct it. The result is the final fifteen-element state vector and the diagonal of the final estimate-error covariance.

feature3_1_measurement_ordering.json.description: Inspect the default field values of a freshly constructed measurement record and the strict ordering predicate used to prioritize measurements by time. A default record has an empty source name and a zero timestamp. The predicate, applied to an ordered pair, reports whether the first argument is strictly newer (later timestamp) than the second; equal timestamps yield a negative result in both directions.

feature3_2_filter_parameters.json.description: Exercise the generic parameter accessors shared by every filter: a debug toggle that only engages when a valid output stream is supplied (and stays disabled otherwise), a sensor-timeout value, a last-measurement-time value, a process-noise covariance matrix and the state vector. After applying the requested values, each is read back. A newly constructed filter reports that it has not yet been initialized.

feature3_3_filter_initialization.json.description: Feed an ordered sequence of full state measurements into the generic measurement-processing routine of a filter that performs no prediction or correction of its own. Before any measurement the filter is uninitialized. The first measurement initializes the state from the measured values that the update mask selects; subsequent measurements advance the last-measurement time. The result reports the initialization status before and after, the resulting state vector and the most recent measurement time.

feature4_geo_utm.json.description: Convert a geographic coordinate given as latitude and longitude in fractional degrees into its projected grid coordinate (northing, easting and grid-zone designator), then convert that projected coordinate back to latitude and longitude. North latitudes and east longitudes are positive. The result reports the projected northing and easting, the grid-zone designator and the round-tripped latitude and longitude.

feature5_state_buffer.json.description: Maintain a fixed-capacity, time-ordered history buffer of estimator states and query it at arbitrary times. Setting a state inserts or overwrites the entry at its timestamp; once capacity is exceeded the oldest entry is dropped. The buffer can be cleared and its current size reported. A query returns a result category — an exact hit, an interpolation between two stored states, or an extrapolation into the past or the future relative to the stored range — together with the resulting state vector at the requested time.
